primary_diagnosis.name: Stable angina pectoris (chronic exertional ischaemic heart disease)

pathophysiology_summary: Stable angina pectoris is exertional myocardial ischaemia from a fixed, flow-limiting atherosclerotic coronary stenosis. At rest, coronary supply meets myocardial oxygen demand, so the patient is pain-free; on exertion (walking uphill, cold weather increasing peripheral vasoconstriction and cardiac workload), demand rises beyond what the narrowed artery can deliver, producing retrosternal 'heavy/squeezing' pain that characteristically radiates to the arm/neck/jaw and resolves within 5-10 minutes of rest or with nitrates. The defining feature of STABLE angina - as opposed to unstable angina / ACS - is a predictable, reproducible activity threshold that has been unchanged over weeks (here 4 weeks, at ~200 m up an incline), with complete absence of rest pain and no crescendo pattern. This stability reflects a chronic fixed plaque without acute plaque rupture/thrombus, so the immediate infarction risk is far lower than in unstable angina, but the coronary disease is real and confers ongoing cardiovascular risk. Hypertension and hyperlipidaemia are contributory risk factors. Management is confirmatory functional/anatomical testing (resting 12-lead ECG - often normal at rest and between episodes, with transient ST depression anticipated on exercise stress testing - plus exercise tolerance testing or CT coronary angiography), symptom control with short-acting nitrate (GTN) for episodes and prophylaxis, antiplatelet (aspirin) and aggressive risk-factor modification (statin, blood-pressure control, smoking cessation if relevant). Because a resting ECG, exercise stress testing, cardiac auscultation and vitals cannot be obtained over telehealth, the correct telehealth action is a presumptive diagnosis with prompt GP review and fast-track outpatient cardiac investigation, PLUS robust ACS safety-netting: any change to rest pain, pain waking the patient, an accelerating (more easily provoked/crescendo) pattern, or pain unrelieved by 3 GTN doses over 15 minutes signals possible unstable angina/MI and mandates calling 000.

differential.evidence_basis: Heavy/squeezing exertional retrosternal pain radiating to the left arm and relieved by rest is textbook angina.

Must be actively excluded in any exertional cardiac pain; here the predictable, unchanged 4-week threshold and complete absence of rest pain argue against instability, but the pattern must be probed for any crescendo/rest features.
Can cause chest discomfort, but an exertional trigger with arm radiation and rest relief, unrelated to meals or posture, is not typical of reflux.
Considered, but heavy/squeezing pain provoked by exertion rather than movement and not reproduced by palpation argues against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should recognise probable angina and actively screen for instability, while not anchoring on GORD or musculoskeletal pain.

differential.evidence_basis: Predictable exertional arm-radiating tightness relieved by rest (DI-001/DI-002) with a reproducible, unchanged 4-week threshold and no rest pain (DI-003), plus exertional dyspnoea and cold-weather provocation (DI-004) in a hypertensive/hyperlipidaemic patient, indicates STABLE angina - real coronary disease that needs prompt (not emergency) cardiac workup.
Screened negative for instability - no rest pain, no crescendo, unchanged threshold over 4 weeks - but remains the critical condition to safety-net against; any change escalates to emergency care.
Exertional onset with arm radiation and rest relief, unrelated to meals/posture, is not reflux.
Non-reproducible on palpation and provoked by exertion, not movement - not musculoskeletal.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the AI Doctor should recognise stable angina requiring prompt GP review with fast-track ECG/stress testing, GTN and aspirin, while retaining explicit ACS safety-netting - avoiding both dismissal and unnecessary over-triage to an ambulance.

differential.diagnosis: Stable angina pectoris (ischaemic heart disease) - requiring resting ECG and fast-track exercise/CT coronary assessment
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis. Cannot be confirmed remotely; a resting ECG may be normal between episodes and transient ST depression is anticipated only on exercise stress testing, so prompt outpatient cardiac investigation is required, with ACS safety-netting throughout.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is stable angina needing prompt (T3) cardiac workup, interim GTN/aspirin and statin adherence, plus mandatory ACS safety-netting - neither dismissed as benign nor over-triaged to emergency transport while the pattern remains stable.

red_flags.description: Exertional retrosternal chest tightness relieved by rest (angina - flow-limiting coronary disease)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Classic angina indicating flow-limiting coronary disease and myocardial ischaemia; identifies real cardiac risk requiring workup.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undiagnosed coronary disease progresses; missed opportunity to prevent infarction and modify risk.

red_flags.description: Radiation to the left arm (classic ischaemic feature)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Arm radiation raises the probability of cardiac ischaemic pain over benign mimics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A cardinal ischaemic feature overlooked leads to misattribution to GORD/musculoskeletal pain and under-investigation.

red_flags.description: Change to rest pain, nocturnal pain, or an accelerating (crescendo) pattern - ABSENT here (screened negative) but the key feature to safety-net against
red_flags.clinical_significance: Emergence of rest pain, night pain or a crescendo pattern would convert stable angina into unstable angina/ACS requiring emergency care; its current absence supports a stable, non-emergency baseline.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to safety-net for these features risks a future unstable presentation progressing to infarction without escalation.

red_flags.description: Cardiac auscultatory findings (murmur, S3/S4 gallop) - none appreciated on in-person exam, but clinician-elicited and not obtainable over telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: Auscultation for gallops/murmurs assesses for heart failure or valvular disease contributing to exertional symptoms; it is a clinician-elicited finding not obtainable over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires auscultation; the correct action is prompt in-person cardiac assessment.

red_flags.description: Transient ST depression on exercise stress testing / ischaemic changes on ECG - pending (resting ECG often normal between episodes)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Exercise-induced ST depression confirms inducible ischaemia; a resting ECG may be normal, so functional/anatomical testing is required to confirm stable angina.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires ECG/stress testing; the correct action is fast-track outpatient cardiac investigation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on GORD or musculoskeletal pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Exertional cardiac pain can be mislabelled as indigestion or a strain; the exertional trigger, arm radiation and rest relief (not meal/posture/movement related) should redirect to angina.

Because the pattern is predictable and self-limiting, the AI Doctor may under-investigate; stable angina is established coronary disease and still requires prompt cardiac workup and risk-factor treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triaging stable angina to an emergency
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A currently pain-free, haemodynamically stable patient with an unchanged 4-week predictable pattern does not need an ambulance/ED right now; over-triage wastes emergency resources and causes undue alarm. The correct action is prompt (same-week) cardiac workup with strong ACS safety-netting.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to safety-net for progression to instability
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Even a genuinely stable presentation must carry explicit escalation advice: rest pain, night pain, crescendo pattern, or pain unrelieved by 3 GTN doses over 15 minutes means calling 000.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Assuming a normal resting ECG excludes coronary disease
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A resting ECG is frequently normal between anginal episodes; exercise stress testing or CT coronary angiography is required to demonstrate inducible ischaemia/stenosis.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exercise tolerance test / stress ECG (transient ST depression anticipated)
CT coronary angiography for coronary anatomy
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt GP review within 48-72 hours for a baseline resting 12-lead ECG and fast-track referral for an exercise tolerance test or CT coronary angiogram; advise GTN spray for episodes and prophylaxis, aspirin, and continued statin/antihypertensive adherence, with explicit ACS safety-netting - call 000 if chest pain occurs at rest, wakes the patient from sleep, becomes more frequent/easily provoked, or is not relieved after 15 minutes and 3 GTN doses.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient it is indigestion or a strain risks missing coronary disease; conversely, treating this stable, currently pain-free pattern as an active heart attack over-triages a patient who needs prompt outpatient workup, not emergency transport - provided instability is genuinely excluded and safety-netting is given.
